summary: Gruber investigates how much money Apple receives from Google for search referrals through Safari. Building on his earlier reporting of a $25 million annual figure from a knowledgeable source, he constructs a rough estimate by working backward from the Mozilla Foundation's public financial disclosures. By comparing Safari's browser usage share to Firefox's and applying Mozilla's known Google revenue, he arrives at estimates ranging from $13 million to $21 million. He suggests the actual number is likely higher because Apple — meaning Steve Jobs — is a better negotiator than Mozilla, and Safari users may be more demographically valuable to Google.
key_points: Mozilla Foundation's Google revenue provides a useful baseline for estimating Apple's deal, since both receive payments for search referrals
Depending on which browser usage share data you trust, Safari has between 20-33% of Firefox's share, yielding Apple revenue estimates of $13-21 million
Safari's search field is hard-wired to Google only, unlike Firefox's configurable search, which could mean Safari sends proportionally more Google referrals
Apple likely negotiates a better per-referral rate than Mozilla, partly because Safari users may be more demographically appealing to advertisers
money_quotes: That number came from, as they say, a source with knowledge of the situation.
One might also presume — as I do — that Apple has better negotiators than the Mozilla Foundation, and that Google pays Apple more per referral than they do Mozilla.
Apple may well argue that Safari users, as a whole, are demographically more appealing than Firefox users.
key_insight: Public financial disclosures from Mozilla can be used to reverse-engineer Apple's Google search revenue, but Apple's superior negotiating leverage likely means the real number exceeds what usage share alone would suggest.
